title: Deep Learning with Differential Privacy
venue: Proceedings of the 2016 ACM SIGSAC Conference on Computer and Communications Security
research_question: Can deep neural networks be trained with a measurable differential-privacy guarantee while retaining useful predictive performance?
method: The paper introduces differentially private stochastic gradient descent with per-example gradient clipping, Gaussian noise, and a moments accountant for tighter cumulative privacy accounting, then evaluates the method on image classification.
population_or_data: Public MNIST and CIFAR-10 image benchmarks used to train and evaluate non-convex neural networks under several privacy budgets.
core_finding: The method trained useful deep models at a stated privacy budget; at epsilon 8 and delta 1e-5, the reported models reached about 97% accuracy on MNIST and 73% on CIFAR-10, with a much larger utility cost on the harder task.
architecture_implications: Treat privacy as an end-to-end, auditable budget attached to a defined privacy unit rather than as a generic claim about encrypted storage.
Clip per-example or per-person contributions before adding noise so a prolific correspondent cannot dominate updates to a personalized model.
Keep the privacy accountant, training configuration, and release decision in the governance plane so every model refresh has a traceable cumulative budget.
limitations: The experiments use public image benchmarks rather than conversational or personalized language data. The guarantee depends on the chosen privacy unit, clipping norm, sampling assumptions, and budget; it does not by itself enforce contextual consent, deletion, or output access control.

search_queries: site:dl.acm.org Deep Learning with Differential Privacy 10.1145/2976749.2978318
arXiv 1607.00133 Deep Learning with Differential Privacy

title: Membership Inference Attacks Against Machine Learning Models
venue: 2017 IEEE Symposium on Security and Privacy
research_question: Can an attacker with only black-box prediction access determine whether a particular record was used to train a machine-learning model?
method: The authors train shadow models that mimic a target model and use their member versus non-member outputs to train attack classifiers, testing local and commercial prediction services and several mitigations.
population_or_data: Classification models and APIs evaluated on CIFAR-10, CIFAR-100, MNIST, UCI Adult, retail purchase records, Foursquare location data, and a Texas hospital discharge dataset.
core_finding: Black-box outputs leaked training membership across multiple tasks and services. Overfitting and class representativeness affected attack success, but neither alone explained vulnerability, and simply returning fewer output probabilities did not consistently remove leakage.
architecture_implications: Run membership-inference audits against every released personalized model, adapter, embedding index, and confidence-bearing API before deployment.
Minimize output detail and rate-limit probing, but do not treat either measure as a substitute for privacy-preserving training and representative evaluation.
Use a per-person training manifest so an owner can determine which artifacts may expose whether a person or conversation contributed.
limitations: The experiments concern 2017-era classifiers and prediction APIs, not modern generative models. The attack assumes a candidate record and suitable reference data, and measured success varies substantially with task, model, and output interface.

search_queries: site:ieeexplore.ieee.org Membership Inference Attacks Against Machine Learning Models 10.1109/SP.2017.41
arXiv 1610.05820 membership inference Shokri

title: Extracting Training Data from Large Language Models
venue: 30th USENIX Security Symposium
research_question: Can an adversary recover verbatim training examples, including personal information, from a large language model through ordinary generation queries?
method: The study generated three sets of 200,000 GPT-2 samples, ranked candidates using six memorization scores, manually inspected 1,800 candidates, and searched the model's documented training corpus to verify memorization.
population_or_data: GPT-2 models trained on WebText, 600,000 generated samples, 1,800 high-ranked candidates, and the corresponding public web training corpus used for verification.
core_finding: The audit verified 604 memorized training examples, including 78 examples containing personally identifiable information. Larger models exposed more memorized text, and extraction did not require obvious global overfitting.
architecture_implications: Place canary, verbatim-match, and PII extraction tests in the release gate for any model trained or fine-tuned on personal communications.
Keep retrieved evidence outside model weights when possible, with authorization checked before each retrieval and with generated output scanned for unsupported verbatim disclosure.
Treat model access as access to a potentially lossy copy of training data; logging, query controls, and incident response must cover extraction attempts.
limitations: The target was the GPT-2 family trained on public WebText, and the untargeted manual audit establishes a lower bound rather than a complete leakage rate. It does not estimate risks for private chat archives, retrieval systems, or later model architectures.

arXiv 2012.07805 training data extraction GPT-2

title: Deduplicating Training Data Mitigates Privacy Risks in Language Models
venue: Proceedings of the 39th International Conference on Machine Learning
research_question: How much of language-model memorization and privacy-attack success is driven by duplicated training sequences, and does deduplication reduce that risk?
method: The authors measure exact-sequence duplication in OpenWebText and C4, train 117M-, 345M-, and 1.5B-parameter language models on duplicated and deduplicated corpora, and compare generation and membership-inference attacks.
population_or_data: A 39 GB OpenWebText corpus, a 750 GB C4 corpus, exact 400-character training sequences, several model sizes, and one million generated samples for the deduplication comparison.
core_finding: Regeneration increased superlinearly with duplication: a sequence appearing ten times was generated roughly 1,000 times as often as a unique sequence. Deduplicated training produced about twenty times less memorized text in the sampled generations and weakened two of three evaluated membership scores.
architecture_implications: Deduplicate exact and near-duplicate personal records before any training split so repeated forwards, quotes, backups, or exports do not amplify one person's contribution.
Track duplicate groups and source provenance so deletion and privacy accounting operate on all copies rather than one row.
Layer deduplication with differential privacy, access control, and extraction testing because unique sensitive text can still be memorized or disclosed contextually.
limitations: The evaluated attacks focus on exact sequences in web corpora and a limited set of model sizes and membership scores. Deduplication is not a formal privacy guarantee, may remove legitimately repeated evidence, and does not address unique PII or inference-time disclosure.

search_queries: site:proceedings.mlr.press/v162 Kandpal Deduplicating Training Data Mitigates Privacy Risks
arXiv 2202.06539 deduplicating privacy language models

title: Analyzing Leakage of Personally Identifiable Information in Language Models
venue: 2023 IEEE Symposium on Security and Privacy
research_question: How much personally identifiable information can black-box extraction, reconstruction, and inference attacks recover from fine-tuned language models, and how well do scrubbing and differential privacy defend?
method: The paper formalizes three PII leakage games and attacks GPT-2 models fine-tuned on legal, email, and health-review corpora under undefended, entity-scrubbed, differentially private, and combined training conditions.
population_or_data: GPT-2 small through XL fine-tuned on 118,161 ECHR case records, 123,317 Enron email records, and 78,794 Yelp health-related reviews, with PII recognized and evaluated across the three domains.
core_finding: The proposed attacks recovered up to ten times more PII sequences than earlier methods. Scrubbing reduced but did not eliminate leakage, while differential privacy sharply reduced it but still left measurable PII exposure in the evaluated settings, partly because privacy units could contain repeated identifiers.
architecture_implications: Evaluate extraction, masked reconstruction, and attribute inference separately; a system can pass one leakage test while failing another.
Define privacy units at the person or relationship level and group repeated identifiers before differentially private training.
Do not rely on automated PII scrubbing alone; preserve a quarantine log of uncertain entities and gate outputs with source-level authorization.
limitations: The study uses GPT-2 and three English corpora, and the same imperfect named-entity machinery contributes to both defense and evaluation. The authors report limited annotation coverage, so measured leakage is an average-case lower bound rather than a complete inventory.

search_queries: site:ieeexplore.ieee.org Analyzing Leakage of Personally Identifiable Information in Language Models
arXiv 2302.00539 PII leakage language models

title: Machine Unlearning
venue: 2021 IEEE Symposium on Security and Privacy
research_question: Can training be structured so that removing a person's data requires retraining only a bounded part of a model rather than rebuilding it from scratch?
method: SISA training shards the data, isolates shard models, slices training into checkpoints, and aggregates predictions; experiments compare unlearning time and accuracy across datasets and request distributions.
population_or_data: Purchase records, SVHN, MNIST, CIFAR-100, ImageNet, and mini-ImageNet classification tasks with simulated unlearning requests.
core_finding: SISA reduced measured unlearning time by 4.63 times on Purchase, 2.45 times on SVHN, and 1.36 times on ImageNet relative to retraining, with accuracy and storage tradeoffs that grew on harder tasks.
architecture_implications: Maintain a data-to-artifact lineage map from source records through shards, adapters, indexes, checkpoints, and releases so a deletion request has an executable scope.
Partition personalized learning by person or source where feasible, then aggregate at inference time to limit the retraining blast radius.
Verify forgetting with post-deletion audits and versioned manifests; deleting a source row is not evidence that derived model artifacts forgot it.
limitations: The evaluation centers on supervised classifiers and simulated requests, not generative models or person-level conversational memories. SISA stores and serves multiple models, and its speedup depends on task complexity, shard accuracy, request distribution, and checkpoint storage.

search_queries: site:ieeexplore.ieee.org Machine Unlearning Bourtoule 10.1109/SP40001.2021.00019
arXiv 1912.03817 SISA machine unlearning

title: Can LLMs Keep a Secret? Testing Privacy Implications of Language Models via Contextual Integrity Theory
venue: The Twelfth International Conference on Learning Representations
research_question: Can instruction-tuned language models preserve contextual privacy when information from one actor is used to answer or summarize for another actor?
method: CONFAIDE evaluates models over four tiers of contextual-integrity tasks: information sensitivity, transmission-principle judgments, three-party secret-sharing scenarios, and meeting-summary disclosure, with human annotations and prompt interventions.
population_or_data: Ten information types, 98 transmission vignettes, 270 three-party scenarios, 20 synthetic meeting transcripts, human ratings for the first three tiers, and multiple commercial and open instruction-tuned models.
core_finding: Even strong models violated human contextual expectations. In the hardest summarization tier, GPT-4 and ChatGPT disclosed designated private information in 39% and 57% of cases, and privacy reminders or chain-of-thought prompting did not reliably fix the behavior and sometimes worsened it.
architecture_implications: Enforce recipient, purpose, and source permissions before context enters the model; do not ask the model to infer authorization from a long prompt.
Represent each memory with sender, subject, audience, purpose, and allowed transformations, then filter retrieval and output against those fields.
Test multi-party flows explicitly, including summaries and replies where one person's information can be exposed to another.
limitations: The hardest tiers use synthetic scenarios and meeting transcripts, human inter-rater agreement was low in parts of the benchmark, participant demographics were not fully reported, and results reflect particular model snapshots. The study measures contextual judgments rather than real-world harm rates.

title: AttriGuard: A Practical Defense Against Attribute Inference Attacks via Adversarial Machine Learning
venue: 27th USENIX Security Symposium
research_question: Can small, user-specific perturbations reduce attribute inference from public behavioral data without destroying the data's utility?
method: AttriGuard finds minimum classifier-evasion perturbations for each possible attribute value, then samples a perturbation through a convex privacy-utility optimization and tests defense-aware and defense-unaware attacks.
population_or_data: 16,238 Google Play users represented by ratings over 10,000 apps, averaging 23.2 rated apps each, with 25 city labels and several attacker and defender classifiers.
core_finding: In the evaluated city-inference task, changing at most four ratings on average reduced several attack accuracies by about 75% and generally caused less recommendation-utility loss than the compared defenses.
architecture_implications: Treat inferred sensitive attributes as an attack surface and measure whether ordinary behavioral features make them recoverable, even when the attributes are not stored.
Prefer suppressing sensitive-feature use or releasing coarse aggregates over modifying canonical personal evidence; any obfuscation layer should be separate, reversible, and disclosed.
Red-team adaptive attackers that can detect or learn around a defense rather than reporting only defense-unaware accuracy.
limitations: The primary experiment protects one static city attribute in a sparse recommender dataset. AttriGuard gives no formal guarantee against an optimal or future adaptive attacker, can alter useful public data, and relies on assumptions about transferability between defender and attacker classifiers.

title: Private traits and attributes are predictable from digital records of human behavior
venue: Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences
research_question: How accurately can sensitive personal attributes be inferred from sparse digital behavior that does not explicitly state those attributes?
method: The study applies singular-value decomposition and logistic or linear models to Facebook Likes, using ten-fold cross-validation to predict demographic, political, sexual-orientation, substance-use, intelligence, satisfaction, and personality labels.
population_or_data: 58,466 United States volunteers from myPersonality, with an average of 170 Facebook Likes, self-reported profiles, survey labels, and psychometric tests; the sample averaged 25.6 years old and was 62% female.
core_finding: Indirect behavioral traces predicted several sensitive labels above chance, including reported AUCs of 0.88 for male sexual orientation, 0.95 for African American versus Caucasian identity, and 0.85 for Democrat versus Republican affiliation.
architecture_implications: Do not create or persist protected-trait fields from behavioral traces unless the person explicitly supplies them for a narrowly defined purpose.
Separate benign personalization from sensitive inference with feature-deny lists, proxy audits, and an abstention path when a requested output would reveal a protected trait.
Explain that removing an explicit profile field does not remove inference risk from likes, language, contacts, and interaction patterns.
limitations: The volunteer sample, 2010s Facebook environment, United States focus, coarse or binary labels, and subgroup imbalance limit generalization. The results are correlational benchmark predictions and do not establish that an inferred trait is accurate, stable, appropriate, or safe for an individual.

title: Gaydar: Facebook friendships expose sexual orientation
venue: First Monday
research_question: Can sexual orientation be inferred from a person's friendship network even when that person does not disclose it?
method: The authors archived public profile and friendship data from one university network and fit logistic models using friends' disclosed orientation signals to predict the focal user's disclosed orientation.
population_or_data: 4,080 public Facebook profiles in the Massachusetts Institute of Technology network, collected in 2007, with friendship links and self-disclosed Interested In fields where available.
core_finding: Friendship structure exposed information beyond a focal profile: the models found predictive network patterns for sexual orientation, especially for men labeled gay, showing that one person's privacy setting cannot contain information jointly encoded by their contacts.
architecture_implications: Treat relationship edges as third-party data belonging to both endpoints, not as attributes the account owner can unilaterally authorize for inference.
Prohibit sensitive-trait propagation across a contact graph and test whether embeddings or nearest-neighbor features reconstruct hidden traits.
Apply group- and edge-level deletion so removing one person's explicit trait also removes derived graph features and cached predictions.
limitations: The study covers one university, public 2007-era profiles, a small labeled subgroup, and narrow platform categories that do not represent the complexity of sexual identity. Predictive association is uneven across groups and must not be treated as permission or identity truth.

title: You Are Who You Know and How You Behave: Attribute Inference Attacks via Users' Social Friends and Behaviors
venue: 25th USENIX Security Symposium
research_question: Does combining a person's social graph with public behavioral records make hidden profile attributes easier to infer than using either source alone?
method: The paper builds a social-behavior-attribute graph and a vote-distribution attack, then compares it with friend-only, behavior-only, and feature-concatenation baselines over repeated train-test samples.
population_or_data: A 2011 Google+ and Google Play snapshot with 1,111,905 users, 5,328,308 social links, 48,706 retained items, 3,635,231 reviews, and 210 curated values across majors, employers, and cities.
core_finding: Combining graph and behavioral evidence improved inference over either source alone. The attack predicted a city for 57% of users and exceeded 92% precision when restricted to the half of targets selected as most confident.
architecture_implications: Audit cross-modal joins, because social edges and innocuous usage traces can jointly reveal attributes that neither table exposes alone.
Keep confidence scores from turning sensitive inference into a product feature; high confidence identifies targets at greater privacy risk rather than making inference authorized.
Use purpose-bound feature views so relationship modeling cannot silently access protected-trait labels or behavior collected for unrelated personalization.
limitations: The Google+ crawl may not represent the full network, only one third of users had review behavior, labels were self-entered and manually consolidated, and only popular majors, employers, and cities were evaluated. The platform and defaults are dated, and the attack's success does not validate individual predictions.

title: Gender Shades: Intersectional Accuracy Disparities in Commercial Gender Classification
venue: Proceedings of the 1st Conference on Fairness, Accountability and Transparency
research_question: Do commercial facial gender classifiers have different error rates across intersections of perceived gender and skin type?
method: The authors construct the 1,270-person Pilot Parliaments Benchmark, annotate perceived binary gender and Fitzpatrick skin type, audit three commercial systems, and report error rates for four intersectional groups.
population_or_data: Constrained official portraits of 1,270 parliamentarians from three African and three European countries, balanced more evenly across darker and lighter skin and perceived female and male categories than prior benchmarks.
core_finding: All three systems performed worst on darker-skinned women; the maximum error in that group was 34.7%, compared with a maximum of 0.8% for lighter-skinned men, demonstrating that aggregate accuracy concealed large intersectional disparities.
architecture_implications: A digital brain should not infer gender from face, voice, name, or writing style; use explicit self-description only when needed and allow no-value, change, and deletion states.
Report errors by relevant intersections before any identity-related feature is considered, and treat missing subgroup coverage as a release blocker rather than averaging it away.
Provide contestability and redress for identity metadata instead of presenting classifier output as a stable fact about a person.
limitations: The benchmark uses binary perceived-gender labels and Fitzpatrick skin type as a phenotypic proxy, contains curated parliamentarian portraits, and tests 2017 versions of three vendors. It does not address nonbinary identity, consent, face recognition, or unconstrained deployment conditions.

title: How Computers See Gender: An Evaluation of Gender Classification in Commercial Facial Analysis and Image Labeling Services
venue: Proceedings of the ACM on Human-Computer Interaction
research_question: How do commercial computer-vision services encode gender, and how do their classifications behave on images self-labeled across diverse gender identities?
method: The paper systematically analyzes ten commercial facial-analysis and image-labeling services, then evaluates five services on a custom dataset built from self-labeled Instagram images representing diverse genders.
population_or_data: A custom collection of self-labeled Instagram images representing diverse genders, ten commercial services in the system analysis, and five services in the empirical evaluation.
core_finding: The services encoded gender through restrictive classification schemas, performed worse for transgender people, and could not represent nonbinary identities when their output vocabulary allowed only binary labels; user expectations also conflicted with what the systems encoded.
architecture_implications: Do not force identity into a model's available labels; preserve a person's own wording and distinguish self-description from system inference.
When an upstream API returns demographic labels, quarantine or discard them by default rather than importing them into long-term memory.
Expose provenance, uncertainty, and a correction path for identity data, with downstream recomputation after correction or deletion.
limitations: This record is based on the publisher abstract and bibliographic metadata. The image sample was self-selected through Instagram labels, the commercial services are 2019 snapshots, and visual presentation is not equivalent to identity; the study does not justify automated gender inference.

title: Exploring interdependent privacy – Empirical insights into users' protection of others' privacy on online platforms
venue: Electronic Markets
research_question: Can making a third party's stake salient reduce a user's willingness to disclose that third party's address-book data to a platform?
method: A between-subject online vignette experiment presents 330 European Instagram users with an actual-style contact-access prompt, tests a combined opt-in, framing, and transparency nudge, and models realization, ownership recognition, and respect as serial mediators.
population_or_data: 330 adult Instagram users in European Union countries responding to a hypothetical address-book access decision and follow-up quantitative and qualitative measures.
core_finding: The interdependent-privacy salience nudge reduced intended disclosure of other people's contact data by 62%. The measured path through realizing a transfer, recognizing others' ownership, and respecting their rights was associated with the decision, although some paths differed from the original framework.
architecture_implications: Consent from the account owner cannot authorize training on or disclosing every correspondent's messages, identifiers, images, or relationship data.
Present third-party scope at import time with explicit opt-in, concrete examples, purpose, retention, and a path to exclude people or fields before ingestion.
Record consent per subject, source, purpose, capability, and time; a single archive-level checkbox is too coarse for interdependent data.
limitations: The experiment measured hypothetical, cross-sectional intent rather than live disclosure. The sample was limited to European Instagram users, the nudge bundled several components, and measured mediators limit causal interpretation; habituation and information overload were not tested.

title: Consent in Crisis: The Rapid Decline of the AI Data Commons
venue: Advances in Neural Information Processing Systems 37, Datasets and Benchmarks Track
research_question: How have web-domain signals about crawling and AI use changed, and how consistently do robots.txt and terms of service communicate those preferences?
method: The authors audit roughly 14,000 domains underlying C4, RefinedWeb, and Dolma, combine automated and human annotations, retrieve historical robots.txt and policy snapshots from 2016 through April 2024, and estimate token-weighted restriction trends.
population_or_data: The top domains in three Common Crawl-derived corpora plus a 10,000-domain random intersection sample, with detailed human annotation for the head set and 2,000 random domains and longitudinal Wayback Machine snapshots.
core_finding: Restriction signals rose quickly and often conflicted: by 2024 more than 5% of all C4 tokens and more than 28% of actively maintained critical C4 sources were fully restricted by measured crawler signals, while terms-of-service restrictions covered 45% of C4 tokens.
architecture_implications: Attach immutable source, collection time, permission signal, and allowed-use metadata to every imported item; re-check mutable permissions before retraining or republishing.
Treat public accessibility, crawlability, model-training permission, personalization permission, and replica generation as separate decisions rather than one public-data flag.
When signals conflict or are absent, quarantine the source or ask for explicit permission instead of converting ambiguity into consent.
limitations: Domain-level robots.txt and terms are imperfect proxies for human consent and can be ambiguous or contradictory. Historical coverage is uneven, policy classification has errors, forecasts assume stable conditions, and the study does not resolve legal enforceability or person-level consent inside public pages.

title: Legacy Contact: Designing and Implementing Post-mortem Stewardship at Facebook
venue: Proceedings of the 2016 CHI Conference on Human Factors in Computing Systems
research_question: How can a social platform design a pre-death delegation mechanism for limited stewardship of a person's profile after death?
method: The paper presents a deployed design case for Facebook Legacy Contact, describing product requirements and implementation choices around advance selection, memorialization, preservation, last requests, and information surrounding death.
population_or_data: The design and implementation process for Facebook's Legacy Contact and memorialized-profile infrastructure; the publisher abstract does not report a separate experimental sample.
core_finding: The design separates stewardship from account impersonation and frames advance designation, memorialization, preservation, and last requests as distinct needs rather than granting a survivor unrestricted control of the deceased person's account.
architecture_implications: Create an advance directive that separately controls preservation, deletion, export, memorial access, and any generative simulation; inheritance of data must not imply permission to impersonate.
Give a named steward narrowly scoped actions, immutable audit logs, and no ability to present generated words as authored by the deceased.
Define an unconfigured default before launch; for a digital replica, the conservative default should be disabled rather than inferred from ordinary account retention.
limitations: This record is based on the publisher abstract and bibliographic metadata. It is a single-platform, company-authored design case with limited evidence about user outcomes, cross-cultural expectations, disputes among survivors, or generative posthumous avatars.

title: Getting Your Facebook Affairs in Order: User Expectations in Post-mortem Profile Management
venue: Proceedings of the ACM on Human-Computer Interaction
research_question: Do people who configure Facebook Legacy Contact and the people they designate share the same expectations about post-mortem profile management?
method: The study uses semi-structured interviews with 30 adult United States Facebook account holders who had either configured their own Legacy Contact settings or been chosen for future delegation by someone else.
population_or_data: Thirty adult Facebook account holders in the United States discussing configuration, communication, closeness, expected responsibilities, and the feature's actual capabilities.
core_finding: Participants tended to choose the Facebook friend they felt closest to and often reciprocated that choice. Account holders and delegates felt confident about communication, yet their expectations did not align with the feature's actual functionality, indicating that quick click-through setup was inadequate for this decision.
architecture_implications: Require capability-by-capability confirmation and a shared preview for both the person and steward, rather than a quick one-sided delegate selection.
Periodically re-confirm the directive and relationship because closeness, wishes, and available capabilities change over time.
Support dispute and pause states when a steward, family member, or platform detects ambiguity; do not let automation turn silence into authority.
limitations: This record is based on the publisher abstract and bibliographic metadata. The small United States sample concerns one Facebook feature and self-reported expectations, not actual bereavement outcomes, contested estates, or interactions with a generative avatar.

title: Exploring public perceptions of artificial intelligence in bereavement support: a qualitative study of griefbots
venue: Frontiers in Digital Health
research_question: How does the public understand the possible benefits, harms, legitimacy, and governance conditions of griefbots that simulate deceased people?
method: A descriptive phenomenological study uses maximum-variation and snowball sampling, semi-structured face-to-face or telephone interviews, and Colaizzi's seven-step analysis to derive themes and governance-relevant subthemes.
population_or_data: Twenty-five adults in Guangdong Province, China, interviewed in October 2024; participants needed a basic understanding of AI resurrection technologies, but bereavement experience was not required.
core_finding: Participants saw the same simulated presence as a possible source of comfort and of dependency, inauthenticity, prolonged grief, stigma, inequality, and posthumous-representation harm. Acceptability was conditional on timing, purpose, vulnerability, consent, and oversight rather than uniformly positive or negative.
architecture_implications: Keep posthumous simulation disabled without explicit advance consent that names the permitted modalities, purposes, audiences, steward, duration, and revocation process.
Always disclose that responses are generated, show source provenance and uncertainty, and forbid claims that the output is a new statement by the deceased.

Add vulnerability-aware safeguards such as usage limits, cooldowns, escalation paths, and a hard pause, while keeping any health or grief-risk interpretation outside the model unless clinically governed.
limitations: Participants described anticipated rather than sustained use of a specific griefbot, came from one cultural region, and were not stratified to compare bereavement histories or vulnerable groups. The qualitative themes cannot establish therapeutic benefit, causal harm, or the effectiveness of proposed safeguards.

title: Proactive Detection of Voice Cloning with Localized Watermarking
venue: Proceedings of the 41st International Conference on Machine Learning
research_question: Can generated speech carry an imperceptible, robust watermark that supports fast detection, localization, and generator attribution?
method: AudioSeal jointly trains a waveform watermark generator and single-pass detector with localization and auditory-masking losses, then tests perceptual quality, edits, cross-domain generalization, localization, attribution, speed, and adversarial removal.
population_or_data: Training on a 4,500-hour VoxPopuli subset; evaluation on LibriSpeech, 10,000-sample VoxPopuli edit sets, ASVspoof, FakeAVCeleb, translated Expresso speech, MusicGen, AudioGen, human MUSHRA ratings, and adversarially attacked clips.
core_finding: AudioSeal achieved an average AUC of 0.97 across tested edits versus 0.84 for WavMark, localized watermarked segments, and detected in one pass up to roughly two orders of magnitude faster. White-box access enabled effective removal attacks, so secrecy of detector weights remained important.
architecture_implications: For synthetic voice generated under the system's control, embed a watermark at generation time where compatible and bind the payload to a model or release identifier; passive deepfake detection alone is not a provenance guarantee.
Preserve an independently signed generation log so a watermark can be checked against who authorized the voice, source consent, purpose, and time.
Assume watermark removal and forgery attempts: rotate keys or detectors, monitor false positives, and never use watermark absence as proof that audio is authentic.
limitations: The watermark only covers generators integrated with the scheme and does not establish consent to use a person's voice. Robustness is empirical over selected edits and attacks; white-box access substantially weakened detection, and deployment requires confidential detector material and false-positive management.

search_queries: site:proceedings.mlr.press/v235 Proactive Detection Voice Cloning Localized Watermarking

title: FaceForensics++: Learning to Detect Manipulated Facial Images
venue: Proceedings of the IEEE/CVF International Conference on Computer Vision
research_question: How well can humans and supervised detectors identify several prominent forms of facial video manipulation under realistic compression?
method: The authors create a benchmark from four manipulation pipelines, apply raw, high-quality, and low-quality compression, compare hand-crafted and neural detectors, run a 204-participant human study, and provide a hidden test benchmark.
population_or_data: More than 1.8 million manipulated images from 4,000 fake videos generated from 1,000 source videos using DeepFakes, Face2Face, FaceSwap, and NeuralTextures, plus 204 mostly computer-science student observers.
core_finding: Human accuracy fell from 68.69% on raw videos to 58.73% on low-quality videos. A face-cropped XceptionNet reached 99.26%, 95.73%, and 81.00% on raw, high-quality, and low-quality benchmark data, but performance varied by manipulation and compression.
architecture_implications: Run likeness outputs through manipulation detection and provenance checks, but require positive authorization and generation records because a detector score cannot establish consent or identity.
Evaluate detectors on unseen generators, codecs, crops, and adversarial post-processing before relying on them for moderation or impersonation warnings.
Provide a visible synthetic-media disclosure and a rapid takedown or contest path for the represented person, independent of detector confidence.
limitations: The benchmark covers four 2018-era manipulation methods and controlled source videos; in-domain accuracy does not demonstrate generalization to later generators or adversarial conditions. Detection addresses artifacts, not authorization, intent, provenance, or whether the depicted person consented.

search_queries: site:openaccess.thecvf.com FaceForensics++ ICCV 2019
10.1109/ICCV.2019.00009 FaceForensics
